bytes.topic: Project Structure — MVC vs vertical slice, feature folders, layering conventions
bytes.title: axum Project Structure — Vertical Slices Beat MVC Every Time
bytes.body:
MVC in axum leads to scattered logic: routes in one file, handlers in another, business logic somewhere else. As your app grows, tracing a single feature requires jumping across four directories. Vertical slices fix this by co-locating everything a feature needs.

Organize by domain: `src/users/`, `src/posts/`, `src/auth/`. Each module owns its `router()`, request/response types, handler functions, and any db queries. The top-level `src/router.rs` simply merges these sub-routers via `Router::merge()` or `Router::nest()`.

Handlers stay thin — they extract data from `State`, `Path`, `Json` extractors, delegate to a service or query function, and return a response type that implements `IntoResponse`. Keep that logic in a sibling `service.rs` or `db.rs` within the same feature folder.

Shared infrastructure (db pool, error types, middleware) lives in `src/infra/` or `src/common/`. Avoid a god-level `handlers.rs`. With vertical slices, adding a new feature means creating one folder and wiring one `Router::merge()` call — nothing else changes.

bytes.topic: Routing — defining routes, route parameters, grouping and prefixing
bytes.title: axum Routing — Parameters, Nesting, and Router Composition
bytes.body:
axum's `Router` is the core routing primitive. Define routes with `.route()`, using `get()`, `post()`, and other `MethodRouter` helpers from `axum::routing`. Path parameters are extracted via `Path<T>` — axum deserializes them directly into your type using serde.

Route grouping uses `Router::new().nest()`, which prefixes all child routes with a given path segment. This is how you organize versioned APIs or feature-scoped routers without duplicating prefixes. Nested routers are composed cleanly at the end — each feature module exports its own `Router`, and they're merged in `main.rs` with `.merge()`.

`nest("/api/v1", user_router)` vs `.merge(user_router)` is a key distinction: `nest` applies a path prefix; `merge` is a flat union. Fallback routes use `.fallback()` on the `Router` itself.

State is passed via `.with_state()` and accessed with `State<T>` extractor — it's available across the entire nested tree without rewiring each route.

bytes.topic: Middleware — request pipeline, global vs route-specific, ordering matters
bytes.title: axum Middleware — Pipeline Order, Global vs Route-Specific
bytes.body:
axum builds its middleware stack on Tower's `Layer` + `Service` traits. Two attachment points control scope: `Router::layer()` wraps the entire router — including 404 responses — while `Router::route_layer()` wraps only the routes registered *before* that call. This distinction is critical: auth middleware on `route_layer()` won't fire on unmatched paths, avoiding accidental short-circuits.

Ordering is outside-in: the last `.layer()` call becomes the outermost shell, meaning it receives the request first. In practice, put `TraceLayer` last (outermost) so every request gets logged before anything else runs. Put compression before tracing if you want accurate byte-count metrics. Auth should sit closest to handlers via `route_layer()` so it only guards real routes.

For custom logic, `middleware::from_fn` accepts an async function with `(Request, Next) -> Response` — no manual `Service` impl needed. Access extensions with `req.extensions()` or inject state via `middleware::from_fn_with_state`. Avoid cloning heavy state inside the middleware fn; use `Arc`-wrapped state passed through `Extension` or `State` instead.

bytes.topic: Input Validation — schema validation at the boundary, sanitization, error responses
bytes.title: axum Input Validation — Schema Validation at the Boundary with validator
bytes.body:
axum doesn't ship built-in validation, but pairing the `validator` crate with a custom extractor gives you schema-level rejection before your handler runs. The pattern: wrap `Json<T>` in a `ValidatedJson<T>` extractor that calls `validate()` on the deserialized payload and short-circuits with a 422 if it fails. This keeps your handlers clean — they receive already-validated data and never see malformed input.

Define your input struct with `#[derive(Deserialize, Validate)]`, annotate fields (`#[validate(length(min = 1, max = 100))]`, `#[validate(email)]`, etc.), then implement `FromRequest` for your wrapper. On validation failure, serialize the `ValidationErrors` map into a structured JSON body — field name as key, error messages as array. Never return raw `validator` error output; normalize it into a consistent `{ field, message }` shape your clients can machine-read.

For sanitization, trim and normalize strings before validation, not after — a leading space shouldn't pass an `email` check only to be stripped silently downstream.

bytes.topic: Dependency Injection — built-in DI containers vs manual wiring, testability impact
bytes.title: axum Dependency Injection — Manual Wiring with State<T> and Why It Beats Magic Containers
bytes.body:
Axum has no built-in DI container — injection is manual, wired through `Router::with_state()`. Your `AppState` struct holds everything: database pools, HTTP clients, service traits. Handlers extract it via `State<AppState>`.

The testability key is trait objects. Instead of concrete types inside `AppState`, store `Arc<dyn UserRepo>`. In production, wire `Arc::new(PgUserRepo::new(pool))`; in tests, swap in `Arc::new(MockUserRepo::new())` — no mocking frameworks, just a struct implementing the trait. Your handler signature never changes.

`FromRef` keeps large state ergonomic: handlers declare only what they need (`State<Arc<dyn UserRepo>>`), not the entire `AppState`. Implement `FromRef<AppState>` for each sub-type and axum performs the split automatically at extraction time.

The tradeoff vs Spring or .NET's `IServiceCollection`: zero runtime reflection, zero startup magic, but you own every wire-up. Circular dependencies are a compile error, not a runtime panic. `AppState` must be `Clone` — which is cheap because it's all `Arc` pointer clones under the hood.

For integration tests, construct the full `Router::with_state(test_state)` — this validates routing, middleware ordering, and handler wiring in one shot.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — global error handlers, consistent error response format
bytes.title: axum — Global Error Handling with a Consistent JSON Response Format
bytes.body: axum doesn't have a single global error handler middleware, but you get the same effect by implementing `IntoResponse` on a custom error type and returning `Result<impl IntoResponse, AppError>` from every handler. Define `AppError` as an enum wrapping domain errors, then implement `IntoResponse` once — serializing a unified `{ "code": "...", "message": "..." }` JSON body with the correct HTTP status. Pair this with a `From<sqlx::Error>` (or other infallible conversions) so the `?` operator propagates errors without boilerplate at the call site. For truly unhandled panics or tower middleware errors, wrap your router with `HandleErrorLayer` from `tower` — axum exposes this via `ServiceBuilder`. This layer catches errors that escape handlers (e.g. timeout, body extraction failures) and lets you map them to the same `AppError` shape. The result: every error path — handler panics, extractor rejections, timeouts — emits a uniform JSON envelope your clients can rely on.

bytes.topic: ORM & Database — query builders vs full ORMs, N+1 prevention, migrations
bytes.title: axum + SQLx — Killing N+1 Queries Before They Kill Your Latency
bytes.body:
SQLx is the default choice for axum apps — async-native, and its `query!` macro catches SQL errors at compile time against your actual schema. SeaORM adds entity relationships and lazy loading, but lazy loading is exactly what causes N+1 problems. In axum handlers, an N+1 emerges when you fetch a list and loop to hydrate related data: one query for posts, then one per post for comments. With 100 posts, that's 101 round trips to Postgres.

The fix with SQLx is a batched `WHERE post_id = ANY($1)` query using Postgres's array operator — a single round trip regardless of list size. Group the results in Rust using a `HashMap`. Avoid SeaORM's `find_related()` inside handler loops for the same reason.

For migrations, embed `sqlx::migrate!()` in your axum startup before `Router::new()` to auto-apply pending migrations from the `migrations/` folder on boot. Pair with `sqlx-cli` (`sqlx migrate add`) for local dev.

Batch at the query layer. Group at the application layer. Never lazy-load in a loop.

bytes.topic: Authentication — JWT validation, session handling, auth middleware patterns
bytes.title: axum JWT Auth — Typed Extractors Over Middleware
bytes.body:
axum's idiomatic auth pattern uses `FromRequestParts` to build a typed extractor that rejects unauthenticated requests before they reach your handlers. Define a `Claims` struct, implement `FromRequestParts<S>` for it, and axum invokes it automatically when a handler declares it as a parameter — no separate middleware layer needed for basic JWT validation.

Inside `from_request_parts`, pull the `Authorization` header, strip the `Bearer ` prefix, then validate with `jsonwebtoken::decode`. Return `StatusCode::UNAUTHORIZED` on any failure; return the decoded `Claims` on success. The handler receives a verified value — no `Option` unwrapping, no extension tower, no threading state manually through layers.

For route-scoped cross-cutting concerns (JWKS cache refresh, per-user rate limiting), use `Router::layer(middleware::from_fn_with_state(state.clone(), auth_fn))`. Scope layers tightly to specific `Router` groups, not the root — public routes like `/health` and webhook endpoints won't be gated. The extractor pattern owns per-handler auth; `layer` owns group-level concerns. Mixing both gives you fine-grained control without polluting every handler signature.

bytes.topic: Security — CORS, rate limiting, security headers, injection prevention
bytes.title: axum Security — CORS, Rate Limiting, and Hardened Headers in One Stack
bytes.body:
axum's `Tower` middleware ecosystem handles most security concerns without reaching for external crates beyond `tower-http`. For CORS, `CorsLayer::new()` gives you per-route origin/method/header control — never use `CorsLayer::permissive()` in production. Security headers come from `SetResponseHeaderLayer` or, more ergonomically, `tower_http::set_header::SetResponseHeaderLayer` combined with `SecureHeaders` from `tower-http`'s `sensitive_headers` module to strip auth tokens from traces.

For rate limiting, `tower::limit::ConcurrencyLimitLayer` caps concurrent connections, but IP-based throttling needs `tower_governor` with an `axum::extract::ConnectInfo<SocketAddr>` extractor feeding a `GovernorLayer`. Injection prevention in axum is primarily about never interpolating `Path`/`Query` extractor values directly into raw SQL — always use `sqlx::query!` macros with bound parameters. Validate all deserialized `Json<T>` payloads with `validator` crate attributes on your request structs so malformed input is rejected at the extractor layer before reaching your handlers.

bytes.topic: Testing — unit vs integration, mocking HTTP, test database strategy
bytes.title: axum Testing — Unit Handlers, Router Integration, and DB Isolation
bytes.body:
axum's architecture makes testing unusually clean. Handler functions are plain async fns — unit test them directly by passing `State(...)`, `Json(...)`, and `Path(...)` extractors without spinning up a server.

For integration tests, convert your `Router` into a `tower::Service` and call `.oneshot(Request)` — no TCP, no port binding. This tests your full middleware stack, extractors, and response serialization in-process. Use `axum::body::to_bytes` to read the response body.

For external HTTP dependencies (e.g. calling GitHub API), use `wiremock::MockServer` — it binds a real local port, returns stubbed responses, and asserts expectations. Pass the base URL through `State` so tests can inject the mock URL.

For database isolation, reach for `sqlx::test` — it spins up a fresh migrated database per test function using a pool fixture, then tears it down automatically. Pair this with a `TestApp` helper struct that holds your `Router` and `PgPool` together, keeping test setup DRY across your test modules.

bytes.topic: Performance — connection pooling, async handlers, response compression
bytes.title: axum Performance — Connection Pooling, Async Handlers, and Response Compression
bytes.body: axum's async handlers run on Tokio, so blocking inside them stalls the runtime. Always use `tokio::task::spawn_blocking` for CPU-bound work and never call synchronous database drivers directly. Pair axum with `sqlx::PgPool` — create one pool at startup, store it in `axum::Extension` or application state via `axum::extract::State`, and pass it into handlers. Pool size should match `max_connections` on your Postgres instance; a common starting point is `PgPoolOptions::new().max_connections(20)`. For response compression, add `tower_http::compression::CompressionLayer` to your router — it negotiates `gzip`, `br`, and `deflate` via the `Accept-Encoding` header automatically. Stack it after `TraceLayer` so compressed bytes don't pollute trace spans. Avoid compressing small responses (under ~1 KB) since compression overhead outweighs savings; `CompressionLayer` lets you set a `minimum_size` threshold. Together, these three changes — pooled DB connections, non-blocking async handlers, and selective compression — address the most common throughput bottlenecks in a production axum service without reaching for a reverse proxy.

bytes.topic: API Design — REST conventions, versioning strategy, OpenAPI generation
bytes.title: axum — REST Versioning and OpenAPI Generation Without the Boilerplate
bytes.body: axum's `Router::nest()` makes URL-based API versioning trivial — nest versioned routers under `/v1`, `/v2` prefixes and share state across all of them via `Router::with_state()`. Each version gets its own handler set without duplicating middleware. For OpenAPI generation, pair axum with `utoipa`: annotate handlers with `#[utoipa::path]`, aggregate them in an `OpenApi` derive macro, and mount the spec via `utoipa_swagger_ui::SwaggerUi`. This avoids manual spec maintenance entirely. One sharp edge: `nest()` strips the prefix before matching, so nested routers must not re-declare the prefix. For header-based versioning (`Accept: application/vnd.api+json;version=2`), implement a custom `FromRequestParts` extractor that parses the header and returns a typed `ApiVersion` enum — axum's extractor system makes this a first-class citizen, not middleware hacks. Prefer URL versioning for public APIs (CDN-cacheable, easy to test with curl) and header versioning for internal service-to-service contracts where you control both ends.

bytes.topic: Serialization — request/response DTOs, camelCase vs snake_case, null handling
bytes.title: axum Serialization — DTOs, camelCase vs snake_case, and Null Handling
bytes.body:
axum delegates serialization entirely to serde, so your DTO shape is defined at compile time via derive macros. By default, struct field names are serialized as-is (snake_case). To emit camelCase for a JSON API consumed by JavaScript clients, use `#[serde(rename_all = "camelCase")]` on the struct — no runtime overhead, no middleware needed.

For null handling, axum's `Json<T>` extractor will reject requests where a required field is missing. Wrap optional fields with `Option<T>` and annotate with `#[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]` to omit nulls from responses instead of serializing them as `null`. To accept missing fields as `None` during deserialization, add `#[serde(default)]`.

Response DTOs returned via `Json(payload)` are serialized automatically — no `.to_json()` call needed. For asymmetric shapes (accept snake_case, emit camelCase), define separate request and response structs rather than fighting serde with multiple attributes on one type. This keeps handler signatures honest and avoids surprises at the boundary.

bytes.topic: Configuration — env vars, settings classes, secrets management
bytes.title: axum Configuration — Typed Settings, Env Vars, and Secrets Without the Boilerplate
bytes.body: axum has no built-in config system, so the idiomatic pattern is a typed `AppConfig` struct deserialized at startup and injected via `State`. Reach for `envy` to deserialize environment variables directly into a struct that derives `serde::Deserialize` — no manual `std::env::var` chains. Pair it with `dotenvy::dotenv()` at the top of `main` to load `.env` in development without leaking it to production. For secrets (DB passwords, API keys), wrap sensitive fields in `secrecy::Secret<String>` — this prevents the value from appearing in `Debug` output or logs. Pass the config into your axum router via `Router::with_state(Arc::new(config))`, then extract it in handlers with `State(config): State<Arc<AppConfig>>`. Avoid storing raw secrets in `AppConfig` fields you'd ever `#[derive(Debug)]` on without `secrecy`. For multi-environment layering (base + override files), swap `envy` for the `config` crate, which supports TOML/YAML/env merging in priority order.

bytes.topic: Logging — structured logs, correlation IDs, request logging middleware
bytes.title: axum Request Logging — Structured Traces and Correlation IDs
bytes.body:
axum delegates request logging to `tower-http`'s `TraceLayer`, which hooks into the `tracing` ecosystem. Add it via `ServiceBuilder` and you get per-request spans automatically. For structured logs, configure `tracing-subscriber` with `fmt::layer().json()` — every `tracing::info!` call within a request span will carry its span fields (method, uri, status, latency) in the emitted JSON.

Correlation IDs require a custom `tower::Layer`. Extract `x-request-id` from incoming headers (or generate a UUID if absent), store it in a `tracing::Span` field via `span.record("request_id", &id)`, and propagate it downstream. Inject the same ID into the response header inside the layer's `call` implementation.

Use `TraceLayer::new_for_http().make_span_with(...)` to customise the root span — add `request_id`, `user_id`, or tenant fields at span creation time so every log line inside that request automatically carries them. Avoid `println!`; use `tracing::info!(status = %resp.status(), "response sent")` so fields stay machine-parseable in production.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — hot reload, code generation, scaffolding CLI
bytes.title: axum Dev Tooling — Hot Reload, cargo-generate, and Scaffolding Your Router Fast
bytes.body:
axum has no official CLI, but the Rust tooling ecosystem fills the gap well. For hot reload, `cargo-watch` with `cargo-watch -x run` restarts your server on file changes — pair it with `tower-livereload` to push reloads to connected browser clients without a full restart. Add `RUST_LOG=tower_http=debug` to surface request traces immediately.

For scaffolding, `cargo-generate` lets you bootstrap an axum project from a template. The community template `https://github.com/rickfast/axum-template` wires up Router, SharedState, and Tower middleware layers out of the box. Run `cargo generate --git <template-url>` and you get a layered `Router` with typed state via `Extension` or `State<Arc<T>>`.

For code generation of repetitive handler boilerplate, `axum-macros` provides `#[debug_handler]` — this isn't scaffolding per se, but it surfaces mismatched extractor errors at compile time rather than runtime, dramatically shortening the feedback loop. Combine all three: generate → develop with watch → debug extractors at compile time.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Install tools
cargo install cargo-watch cargo-generate

# Hot reload: restart server on any src change
cargo watch -x run

# Hot reload with browser push (add to Cargo.toml: tower-livereload)
# In main.rs:
let app = Router::new()
    .route("/", get(handler))
    .layer(LiveReloadLayer::new());

# Scaffold a new axum project from template
cargo generate --git https://github.com/rickfast/axum-template --name my-api

# Enable compile-time extractor diagnostics
#[axum::debug_handler]
async fn handler(State(db): State<Arc<Db>>) -> impl IntoResponse {
    StatusCode::OK
}

bytes.topic: Monitoring — health check endpoints, metrics exposure, distributed tracing
bytes.title: axum Observability — Health Checks, Prometheus Metrics, and Distributed Tracing
bytes.body: axum pairs naturally with Tower's middleware ecosystem to wire up production observability in minutes. For health checks, expose a `/health` route returning 200 JSON — liveness and readiness variants let Kubernetes differentiate between "restart me" and "stop sending traffic". For metrics, `axum-prometheus` wraps the `metrics` crate and auto-instruments every route with request count, latency histograms, and in-flight gauges via a `PrometheusMetricLayer` inserted into your `Router`. Scrape `/metrics` with Prometheus and you get per-route `axum_http_requests_total` labels out of the box. For distributed tracing, `tower-http`'s `TraceLayer` generates spans for every request. Pair it with `tracing-opentelemetry` and an OTLP exporter to propagate `traceparent` headers across services. The critical detail: insert `TraceLayer` before `PrometheusMetricLayer` in the layer stack so trace context is available when metrics middleware runs. All three concerns compose without touching handler code — pure Tower layer separation.

bytes.topic: Background Jobs — task queues, scheduled jobs, worker process patterns
bytes.title: axum Background Jobs — Channel-Based Task Queues with Tokio Workers
bytes.body:
axum runs on Tokio, so background jobs are first-class citizens — no separate job runner needed for most workloads. The canonical pattern: thread an `mpsc::Sender<T>` through axum's `State` extractor and spawn a worker loop before starting the server.

Handlers enqueue work via `state.job_tx.send(payload).await` — non-blocking from the HTTP path. The worker task, spawned with `tokio::spawn`, drains the channel independently of request handling. Use a bounded channel (`mpsc::channel(n)`) in production — it applies backpressure and prevents silent OOM under burst load. Unbounded channels are a footgun.

For scheduled jobs, `tokio-cron-scheduler` runs inside the same Tokio runtime — no separate process. For durable queues with retries and persistence, `apalis` integrates directly with axum's `State` and supports PostgreSQL and Redis backends out of the box.

Graceful shutdown is non-negotiable: store the `Sender` in your axum state and drop it when a shutdown signal arrives. The worker's `rx.recv()` returns `None` and exits cleanly. Wire this into `axum::serve(...).with_graceful_shutdown(signal)` to drain in-flight jobs before the process terminates.

bytes.topic: Framework-specific — Django batteries-included, FastAPI auto-docs, NestJS decorators, Phoenix LiveView
bytes.title: axum's Custom Extractors — Your Framework-Level Batteries
bytes.body:
Unlike Django's batteries-included auth or NestJS decorators, axum ships lean — but its `FromRequestParts` trait lets you bolt on the same ergonomics yourself, with full type safety baked in at the handler signature level.

Implement `FromRequestParts` on any struct and axum will automatically call it before invoking your handler. This means JWT validation, tenant resolution, feature flags, or rate-limit context can all live as zero-boilerplate extractor arguments — no middleware plumbing into `Extension`, no manual `req.extensions().get::<T>()` unwrapping.

The key distinction from other frameworks: rejection is typed. Your extractor returns `Result<Self, Self::Rejection>`, where `Rejection` implements `IntoResponse`. A failed auth check short-circuits the handler and returns your exact error shape — no exception handlers or global error middleware needed.

Compose extractors freely: `ValidatedJson<T>` can wrap `Json<T>` internally, adding Serde deserialization *and* `validator` crate checks in one shot. Stack `AuthUser` + `ValidatedJson<CreatePostRequest>` in a single handler signature and axum resolves both in order, failing fast on the first rejection.
